ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKL covers raw veal meat broadly; no specific loin-chop sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 hierarchy. Loin chop specificity documented in notes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical bone-in veal loin chop weighs approximately 150 g, providing around 265 kcal, 30 g protein, and 15 g fat before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Veal, loin, chop, separable lean and fat, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17196)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw veal loin chop (separable lean and fat) used as primary nutrient anchor. HPB Singapore values (energy 177 kcal, protein 20.07 g, fat 10.07 g, carbohydrate 0.07 g, sodium 98 mg, potassium 241 mg, calcium 14 mg, iron 0.84 mg, cholesterol 59 mg) are consistent with USDA data and are retained as the primary per-100g values. Saturated fat derived from USDA SR Legacy for the same cut. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values sourced from USDA FoodData Central for raw veal loin.

gi_source.title: N/A — Veal loin chop, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.07 g/100 g)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Veal loin chop contains negligible carbohydrates (0.07 g/100 g). GI testing is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. No meaningful GI value is assigned per standard practice for protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy template numeric type requirement, with gi_category 'not_applicable' documenting that GI assessment does not apply to this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (98 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium (241 mg/100 g) and saturated fat below 5 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Veal loin chop is suitable for hypertension management due to its low sodium content (98 mg/100 g) and a sodium-to-potassium ratio below 1; trim visible fat before cooking and avoid high-sodium marinades or sauces to maintain this favourable profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.07 g/100 g) and zero sugar; high protein supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veal loin chop has negligible carbohydrate content (0.07 g/100 g) and zero sugar, making it an appropriate protein source for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-sugar glazes or sauces during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.81 g/100 g and cholesterol 59 mg/100 g; red meat classification warrants moderation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Veal loin chop contains moderate saturated fat (3.81 g/100 g) and cholesterol (59 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g, trim all visible fat, prefer grilling or baking over frying, and consume red meat no more than 1–2 times per week in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (bovine/calf) classification; arachidonic acid (omega-6) content amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines per EULAR guidelines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Veal loin chop is classified as red meat and contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids) that may amplify inflammatory pathways in rheumatoid arthritis; limit consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week per EULAR RA guidance, trim all visible fat, and prefer anti-inflammatory protein sources such as fatty fish or legumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veal loin chop, raw, is a bone-in cut of pale red meat taken from the loin section of a young calf (typically under 6 months of age). The raw meat displays a characteristic light pink to pale red colour with a tender, fine-grained texture and mild flavour compared to mature beef. Each chop includes the loin muscle with internal and external fat attached and a central bone. Nutritionally, per 100 g raw weight, it provides approximately 177 kcal, 20.1 g protein, 10.1 g fat (of which 3.8 g is saturated), and only 0.07 g carbohydrate with zero sugar, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. Sodium is low at 98 mg/100 g and potassium is 241 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio for blood pressure management. Iron content is 0.84 mg/100 g and cholesterol is 59 mg/100 g. Veal is widely used in European cuisines, particularly Italian (e.g., vitello) and French (e.g., côtelette de veau), and is prepared by grilling, pan-frying, braising, or roasting. Clinically, veal loin chop is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management due to its negligible carbohydrate and low sodium content, but should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and red meat classification. Glycemic index is not applicable given negligible carbohydrate content.

Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: detailed vitamin D data for raw veal loin chop not available in HPB Singapore database; USDA FoodData Central reports negligible/not-determined values for this specific cut. Food subgroup corrected from 'Beef' (input) to 'Veal' as this is young calf meat, taxonomically distinct from adult beef. Halal status noted as conditional — veal must be slaughtered according to halal requirements; the dietary tag is preserved from the input record. Kosher status not confirmed as veal requires specific kosher slaughter and preparation; is_kosher set to false pending verification.
